text.heading: Debtors roll
text.p: In a move akin to disrobing the masquerade in the public square, the Senate took the unusual step of naming erstwhile directors and chief executives of failed banks involved in N53.3 billion insider loans abuse. The amount is apparently the outstanding sum after the Nigeria Deposit Insurance Corporation’s (NDIC) recovery of N4.722 billion, which the former directors gave out in loans to the companies where they had interests.

The Senate is also reported as pressing to create special courts to try failed banks’ loan defaulters, as a measure to bring succour to innocent depositors of the banks, said to be owed a whopping N188 billion.

We welcome the Senate’s bold move to revisit a matter over which many innocent Nigerians not only lost their entire life savings, but in which some actually died in the long wait for restitution from the NDIC, after those banks were ran aground by their owners and managers.

As unsettling as naming names is to some, its significance lies in its ripping the anonymity shield behind which the actors took shelter, after killing their institutions. We consider it a first step in the quest to deal with the impunity of the era in which ownership of a financial institution, and the privileges that went with it, became license to feather individuals’ business interests, to the detriment of the entire financial system.

At this stage, we can only consider the job only as half done, given what we know of the elite penchant for diversionary, hair-splitting legalese. We suspect that a lot of noise would be made around the so-called legal or ethical issues of making names of the debtors public, as if those issues are far more important than the duty of making good the payment of the debts owed.

The nation has apparently come full circle from the 1990s when the late General Sani Abacha herded distressed banks’ chiefs to jail for the same reason of insider-credit abuses, albeit, without the rigours of due process. In the past decade, the system appears to have lapsed into the other extreme where impunity is condoned and where criminal violators of rules and regulations are rewarded with public appointments.

We therefore see the need to bring a closure to the sordid chapter as an imperative, to restore integrity into the system and to send the signal that such ignoble conduct would never escape sanctions. The Senate has taken an important step in this regard. Pressure must be made on debtors to pay off what they owe by whatever means legal and constitutional, including the possibility of a blacklist from the financial system, when all suasions fail.

Do we need special courts to try the alleged offenders? The sentiment behind the recommendation is understandable, given the belief that the slow wheel of the administration of justice may impede the process of their prosecution and, the objective, which is, restitution. However, the point is that we haven’t yet seen evidence of diligence on the part of either the NDIC or even the apex bank to prosecute the alleged offenders, to convince on the need for special courts.

The lesson of the failed banks’ loan defaulters, yet again, reminds of the need for the regulators, particularly the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) and the NDIC to be alive to their duties of regulation. That the nation is saddled with pervasive operators’ delinquencies is only to be explained by the failures of the regulatory institutions.

This is where the declared resolve of the CBN governor, Sanusi Lamido Sanusi, to be proactive in regulation makes eminent sense. We must say never again to the financial system pathology in which operators would run institutions aground while helping themselves to depositors’ funds.
